title: Postselection-free ballistic-diffusive transition in monitored spin chains
authors: K. G. S. H. Gunawardana, Ali G. Moghaddam, Teemu Ojanen
abstract: We study spin and entanglement dynamics in spin-1/2 XXZ chains under periodic monitoring and show that this system exhibits two measurement-induced phase transitions: a steady-state entanglement phase transition similar to those in monitored quantum circuits and a ballistic-to-diffusive transition in transient dynamics. Specifically, we discover that at low monitoring rate, an initial configuration containing a domain wall $|\uparrow\uparrow\uparrow\ldots \downarrow\downarrow\downarrow\ldots\rangle$ spreads ballistically while, at large monitoring rates, the domain melting is diffusive. Extensive numerical simulations, supported by theoretical arguments, indicate that the ballistic-diffusive transition is intimately interlinked with the entanglement phase transition. In contrast to the entanglement phase transitions, which require exponentially complex postselection, the ballistic-diffusive transition can be observed without postselection and constitutes an experimentally accessible manifestation of the many-body Zeno effect.
summary: This paper demonstrates that monitoring a spin chain induces both an entanglement phase transition and a transition from ballistic to diffusive transport. Crucially, the transport transition can be observed through linear observables without the need for the complex postselection required for entanglement entropy. This provides a practical pathway for studying measurement-induced phenomena in quantum simulators.
main_problem: Investigating the connection between measurement-induced entanglement phase transitions and transitions in transport dynamics (ballistic-to-diffusive) in monitored spin chains.
main_result: The study discovers a postselection-free ballistic-to-diffusive transition in the transient dynamics of spin chains that is closely interlinked with the steady-state entanglement phase transition.
method: Numerical simulations using Matrix Product State (MPS) methods with the ITensorMPS package, involving trajectory averaging and single-parameter scaling analysis.
model_or_system: One-dimensional spin-1/2 XXZ chains in the gapless regime subject to periodic monitoring (measurements) in the high-frequency regime.
key_observables: Von Neumann entanglement entropy, domain-wall width (spatial variance of magnetization), and local magnetization density.
important_parameters: Measurement rate (p), anisotropy parameter (Delta), and system size (L).
main_assumptions: The unmonitored limit derivation assumes the light cone remains far from system boundaries; the high-frequency monitoring is treated in the discrete-time limit.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the domain wall melting process; Figure 2 illustrates the Trotter-decomposed dynamics; Figure 3 displays entanglement entropy growth and transitions; Figure 4 shows the evolution of the spatial variance and its derivatives; Figure 5 presents the scaling analysis.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of measurement-induced transitions, describes the XXZ model and monitoring protocol, presents numerical results for entanglement and transport dynamics, provides scaling analysis and theoretical arguments for the link between transitions, and concludes with experimental implications.
why_interesting: It identifies a way to observe measurement-induced phase transitions using linear, postselection-free observables, providing an experimentally accessible manifestation of the many-body Zeno effect.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Ballistic to diffusive transition in periodically mon- itored spin chains. (a) Domain-wall initial state evolving un- der monitored dynamics. Unitary blocks (shown in purple), U = e−iHδt, and measurement steps each correspond to a short time interval δt ≪1. The probability of a measurement oc- curring at each measurement block is therefore pδt ∼p δt. At later times, the domain wall melts, leading to a broader tran- sition region between the fully up- and down-polarized do- mains. (b) Spreading of the initial sharp domain wall to a smoother profile quantified with a width σ(t). At low moni- toring rate p < pc, the dynamics is ballistic (σ ∝t), while for p > pc it is diffusive (σ ∝ √

FIG. 2. Dynamics of spin chains in the high-frequency moni- toring regime. (a): Temporal evolution consist of alternating unitary layers and measurement layers. (b): 2nd order Trot- ter decomposition of the unitary layer. The two-qubit gates correspond to e−ihi,i+1δt/2.

FIG. 3. Entanglement dynamics and entanglement phase transitions. (a)-(d): Entanglement entropy as a function of time for different monitoring strengths p. The time-evolution has been calculated by employing time step δt = 0.1. In the small p regime we employ the maximum MPS bond dimension χ = 350, while for p > 0.2, the convergence of the entanglement entropy is reached when χ ≈50. (e)-(g): Trajectory-averaged entanglement entropy as a function of p. The results are obtained by averaging over 500 random trajectories at each p ≠0. Additionally, the results have been averaged over the time window 60 < t < 100 where the average value has been saturated.

FIG. 4. Temporal spreading of the spin up domain for different monitoring strengths. (a): Dots correspond to the actual data points and the dashed line represents the spline fitting. (b): Derivative has been obtained by differentiating the spline fit in (a). (c): Second derivative is obtained by differentiating a spline-smoothed first derivate curve.

FIG. 5. Scaling analysis of the magnetization dynamics. (a): The effective spatial variance of the magnetization domain as a function of monitoring rate at different times. (b): Scaling collapse of the curves in (a) with the scaling form (16) and the critical parameters pc = 0.15, β = 1.3 and η = 2.5.
